speaker: interviewee
text: It's because Mr. Spielberg have no idea what is a concentration camp. If he thinks that was what you had in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the movie.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the movie, then he's--

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: --mistaken. Of course, this is not You have seen the movie that's called

speaker: interviewer
text: I saw part of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of it? What is-- now, but of course, if he would have made a very accurate account of the concentration camps, he wouldn't have received that many viewers. People want to see this because this is just about enough. Do you-- you don't, no? You don't do it yet?

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not on tape yet? We're not filming yet, are we?

speaker: interviewer
text: I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. I-- I didn't realize it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just something aside, some part about I'm not knocking it. I'm just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we still rolling?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I'm rolling now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this tape 3, January 9th, 1995. The survivor is Mr. George Meles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I told you, we had been delivering some ammunition to the Hungarian soldiers. And in-- in the meantime, we got an attack by air from somebody-- who knows. It has-- may have been English, French, American, or Russian. The chances were that we got a Russian attack, because we were bombarded with about a fist size-- a little bit larger than fist-sized bombs. And those bombs didn't explode. So we were very safe. All what we wanted that those bombs shouldn't hit us on the head, because then we would have had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So otherwise, we were very safe because-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't remember. We had been attacked two times by the Russians. And I don't recall any explosion at all. And they were small ones anyway. I just didn't know what they tried to achieve by the small attacks. I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was it. Did they give you any protection to wear when you were sent to the front? The Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we weren't. We weren't provided with anything. Whatever my parents sent me, that was it until the end of the war. And when-- now, in the meantime, when we were in Stanislau, still on the same city, which one we used as a base camp, we have been sent over to the Germans for a day or two. You know the Russians had wider rails railway, wider rails than the Europeans. So in order for the Germans to use the wider rails, they had to make it narrower.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were unscrewing this, the rails, and push them together. And then we screwed it on again. One of these days, one of those days, one of the German soldier who was giving us instruction in beautiful German, which most of the kids-- I did understand it. But most of the Hungarian Jewish kids didn't understand. And then he became [LAUGHS] agitated because he was saying what to do and nobody did it because they just didn't know what the heck is he talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went berserk. And he started to kick and-- and beat people-- the Germans. And we just-- I was one of those lucky fellows who was kicked by him on the shin bone. And I had a big pain. And when we could-- I could hardly walk back to my quarters-- wherever we were staying, the group of people. And the following day, it started to swell. And then the second, third, and fourth day, it was swelling and became larger and larger. This is-- this was my calf on the left side. And it became-- it was ballooning up. And surprisingly, instead of being shot at-- at our quarters, I was taken to a Hungarian hospital, which operated in Stanislau on Hungarian soldiers-- Gentile ones, not Jews. And I was taken there as a Jew. But there was no difference at the time. The only difference between me and the Gentile soldiers that the Hungarian soldiers, not the doctors, have taken away everything that I  had. I had some cigarettes, because I wasn't smoking. I was saving it. For cigarettes, you could get favors. Many times you could change it. And for cigarettes, you can get bread. You could get clothing. You could get a lot of favors. So the first thing what they did, they have taken away. And I had a sleeveless vest which was made of lamb-- lamb, not lamb's wool. It was real [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] lamb skin. And it was taken away. And I said to them, if you are take it away, I'm going to freeze during the next winter. And they say, well, they don't really care about it  if I'm going to be frozen. And they took it away anyway. So I was taken to-- to the surgery in-- which was in a huge, big room. The way I remember the room was as-- as big as a-- as a gymnasium in Canada, which it may have been a gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were tables on top of table, like next to each other on both sides. And I was put-- I was rolled in on-- on something. And then I was put on the operating table. And the minute they put it on the American operating table, my swollen calf just burst open. And they started to-- to bleed.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very dark blue. And it most probably, it was blood with pus mixed. And I was put to sleep. And when I was-- when I woke up, it was all done. And I have had a kind of-- it looked like a plastic tube. I don't know if in those days they used plastic tubes, which was put across my-- my feet, which had holes on both sides and-- and inside which one was supposed to clear the pus, which was left over. And in-- in the meantime, as I recollected, I was yelling that somebody kicked me. And-- and that what it is. It was more or less in a delirium. I was to put to sleep at a time when it has taken effect or afterward. I just was talking sense-- which one didn't make sense to anybody else, just for me. Now, before this happened, I had the first bath in-- in almost from September until June. It's more than half a year-- the very, very first bath. And I got rid of the lice. So I had some-- I just benefited of this kick in my shinbone. Actually, most probably, this has saved my life. I was put on-- in about two days later, I was put on a train. They called it a hospital train. Everybody was hurt, sick, amputated. And I was sent to Hungary. But before Hungary, there was another small community, which one had a Hungarian, again, a Hungarian hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you with Jews and non-Jews that were being transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: I what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you, as a Jew, were you with non-Jews also transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I tell you something. When somebody is dead, he's dead. It doesn't make if a person is Jewish or Gentile. But I still-- so I'm still surprised that they bothered to send a Jew on a-- on a military hospital train to another place where I was taken off. And I was carried, transported by horse-drawn carriage from the railway station to the-- to the hospital. And the road wasn't paved. And every time we went over a bump, I had terrible pain because I was just freshly operated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was very, very, very painful. But who am I to complain about it? So I arrived to that hospital where the Jews were separated from the Gentiles. The Gentiles were in a multi-story building-- one or two-story building. And we were at the other end of-- of that ground in a kind of a flat, one-story wooden building. So the doctors came to visit me the following day. What was the name of this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. It was just a place-- a place very close to the Hungarian border. And they had decided that the swelling is coming back. So instead of taking the easy way out and shoot me, they have taken me back to the operating room. And they operated on me  a second time. This time, they put a kind of plastic tube, which I was facing down. So this way, the pus, the mix, the blood and pus had a chance to-- to escape through the pus thing. But I think when they were operating me, they got rid of most of the pus anyway. It was a very, very painful, because seemingly, the people who were giving me the anesthetic wasn't really a skillful person. And he put the anesthetic into that part of the leg which one was swollen and was full of pus. Of course, when you get an anesthetic into the pus, it doesn't work. It has to be put into the flesh, into something solid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I got back to the room where the rest of the soldiers, Jewish forced laborers were lying, asked me, did they try to kill you? You were yelling somewhat awful. Of course, I was somewhat awful because they were operating me without any anesthetic. The anesthetic which one they have given me just didn't work. So I was taken-- I was there for about two more weeks. And then I was shipped back to Hungary in which one is [NON-ENGLISH], which one was Hungarian at that time when Hitler donated it to the Hungarians. And that was a place called Munkacs. And that's M-U-N-K-A-C-S. Now, they call it Mukacheve. Now, it belongs to the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in that hospital for two and a half months. For the first two, three weeks, I was really in bad shape. But little by little, the foot-- the leg, not the foot, the leg, has-- has improved. And I could walk about-- after about two or three weeks. And I didn't have any trouble at all. But miraculously, I wasn't sent away from the hospital. We were kept there for two and a half months. And we never seen a doctor. We just got food regularly. We were sleeping in a bed. And all what we didn't do was go on the street. But we could see from the-- from the window. And we didn't show off that we can walk. The other people were complaining about something else. But there were about, I would say, about 25 people in that big hospital room. If it was a big room. And nobody was sent out seemingly. We got some very good Hungarian doctors there who just realized it if they are going to send us out, we may die. And that they just didn't do this-- didn't want to do this. I don't know why. After this two and a half months, the Russian were on the Hungarian border. And they were pushing the German and Hungarian armies back through the Carpathians into Hungary proper. And we have seen it as they were coming. It was a beautiful sight. The-- the Germans were coming on foot. And they were bedraggled. They were-- their clothing was in pieces. And we knew that we were not going to stay there forever. Now, at that point, we were sent back to [INAUDIBLE], which was in deeper in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that also a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was also--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was also a hospital. And we had been there about two weeks or so. And there was a man who was from our city, Budapest, who knew my father. And when we had arrived, my name must have been registered at-- at the office of the hospital. And he signed there. He was working for the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he thought it was my father who is there. So he came to see me. And he said, oh, Meles, you are not the one I'm looking for. Where's your father? I said, my father is in Auschwitz and most probably is not alive anymore. He said, well, do you have some money someplace in-- back in the city? I said, I think so. You can go. And I-- I think I can tell you where to go. He says, well, I offer you something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to bring you money. Give me a piece of paper. And I'm going to go to the Gentile woman who had the money, because my father before they were taken away had given quite a bit of money to that Gentile woman who had a Jewish husband. The husband was in forced labor camp or someplace else. And he says, well, if you want the 500 Hungarian pengo, which was a lot of money at that time, I keep half and you keep half. So if there was such a generous person who was willing to split some money with me, [LAUGHS] all that he had to do was just about three hours-- he went away. Every weekend, he went back to our city every weekend. So it wasn't a special effort for him to go and ask for the money. I gave him the paper. And I said the 500 pengo-- I authorize this person to take and signed it. And then he came back. He was an honest man. He didn't keep the 500. He just kept 250. I met this man after the war. And he was telling me what a good man he was. And I just didn't want to see him. I just didn't want to talk to him-- a person who has taken 50% of the money for two hours [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ride on the railway. It wasn't a person who deserved any thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, anyway, we have received in [INAUDIBLE] a piece of paper which we were supposed to show. And I was supposed to look for my unit. How do I know where my unit is? I let a unit in-- in June in Poland. And I was in Hungary about four and a half months later. How do I know where it is? They didn't have me. Nobody knew anything. They were just running like crazy, because the Russians were coming at a very rapid space.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I had a chance to escape. There were some kids-- some soldiers, some forced laborers-- who went into-- into small huts where the peasants were keeping utensils, you know, like spades and all sorts of other tools. And they were trying to stay there and wait until the Russians will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't have the courage to do this. And I chose instead to go to Budapest on a-- on-- on a railway by-- by train. OK? By train. And I was traveling for four days on-- what do you call it? Where you get on the train and before you go into the wagon, there is a space. I don't know what is the name of that-- that place. We call it in Hungarian [HUNGARIAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- and I just had one corn, a big cornbread. The big cornbread I hated. I just don't like cornbread. So I wasn't eating for one day. The second day, I was tasting the cornbread. And I said, well, it's not too bad. I'll eat a little bit. The second day, I ate half. And the last day, I ate the whole thing. It just shows if you don't like something, that is a chance. Either you-- you die of hunger or you're going to eat it whether you like it or not. At the same time some time ago, the-- the friend with whom I went to school and I became-- we became buddies during the forced labor camps was-- had decided that he is going to go over to the Russians. So he was hiding someplace and waited until the Russians came. And when the first Russian soldiers came-- arrived to that point where he was hiding, he gave himself up. And the Russians have taken him into a camp-- the Russian concentration camp instead of a German concentration camp. And he was in Russia from 1944 until 1951 for six solid years. He was a Jew who was trying to escape from the German-- German concentration camp-- got into the Gulag.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it shows-- there's a saying to jump out of the pan into the flame, or something like that. So he was doing this. So I cannot really complain that I didn't take a chance because the result was very bad. Now, eventually I arrived to Budapest. I arrived about October 15. October 15 was the day when Horthy, the Hungarian ruler at that time, decided that war is going to be over for the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he made an announcement through the radio reporter. He-- he still didn't speak Hungarian at that time. So one of my friends with whom I graduated from high school was the one who he and his-- and another radio announcer who was telling the Hungarians that the war is over. Now, the interesting thing that I-- at that time, I arrive-- when I arrived to Budapest, the only solid point was my wife's aunt and uncle. And I visited them over in a Hungarian-- in a Jewish apartment building where the Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Arrow Cross Party. That was the-- this was the fascist party who has checked everybody twice a week-- twice a day-- anytime whenever they did that, so they checked if there was anybody else living in those apartments or only the people who were entitled to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were very much afraid when I have shown up, because they were afraid-- supposed they come, the whole-- both them and I were going to be executed or taken over away. I was already taken away. So where could they take me away? How-- how-- how big was the ghetto by then? Or how small was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't-- in those days, it was in Budapest. The Hungarian ghetto ceased in July when my parents were taken and my wife was taken. But there were certain-- in Budapest, the Jews were not deported. Budapest was the last place where they have taken the Jews. And all the country was free of Jews at the time, except the forced labor Jews and the people of Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were certain apartment buildings where they were concentrating Jews. And they could go out certain time of the day to buy something if they had money to buy it with. And I was-- I was given a lunch. Just to illustrate how stupid I was, we had a complete lunch. And at the end of the lunch, there was corn on the cob. Of course, I love corn on the cob. But I, as a well-behaved boy, said, oh, thank you, thank you. I don't eat anymore. This was enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, of course, later on, I was very sorry that I have refused the corn on the cob. It was just upbringing, stupid upbringing. And so I went to a-- they were telling me that there is a group of Jews. As I remember, they were older Jews who were-- who must have had a special influence on the military. They were kept in the-- in the synagogue. And they were so loosely kept that they could have gone home to sleep with their family. They come back to the following morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a very nicely arranged situation. In Hungary in those days and-- and ever, you could achieve a lot of things with money. So those people were monied people who-- who had these privileges. So I was so tired, and of course, they had-- they had something like a bed. They were carrying from home duvets. And they were coming. And it was just-- it was like having a holiday. So I went there. And I got into my sleeping bag. And I went to sleep. And I was that dead tired. For four days, I was traveling on the train. By the way, on the train, there were Ukrainians in German service. They were in uniform. And it was written Ukrainian and German service on the side of the arm with their girlfriends. They didn't bother me. Did you have identification that said you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I'll come to it. I was in just my regular outfit, which was a waterproof jacket. Plus, of course, on my back, there was the sleeping bag. There was a Hungarian fellow that was next to the engineers wagon. And he was looking at me. And eventually he came over, and he says, you are Jew. I said, yes. He said, where is your yellow band with a star? I said, in my pocket. He says, now get it out dog and put it on. So he told me the Ukrainians are going to cost me some trouble. I put it on. And they didn't bother. They didn't care, because they were just worried. They were running away from-- from the Russian Army, because if they were caught, most-- probably most of them would have been shot dead as deserters. Deserters?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was another reason the Hungarian loved me. And from that place, as I remember, during the night I was half asleep. And they were standing telling-- talking to each other. The one said-- there was a Gentile Hungarian man who said, take off that the yellow star. You don't need it anymore. The war is over. So I remember the war was over. That's on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on the train. I was in the-- the synagogue where the Jews were kept. And they were telling each other the war is over. So OK, but I was too tired to celebrate. I went back to sleep. And in the morning I woke up, and the war-- war was on again. The extreme rightest fascists have taken over the-- the rule. The Germans have sent in some tanks. I think all together there were two tanks which went around and around on the main street of Budapest to intimidate the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And later on, I got into a kind of a group of people who went to do work for the SKF. You know the ball bearing people from Sweden? They had SKF I think is the name. They were having a lot of stuff in Pest. Now, they thought that Buda, which was a mountainous place of the capital is going to be taken later or not at all by-- by the Russians, because the Russians were coming from Stalingrad. Certainly, they can't cross over the Danube. So they weren't mistaken of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: For six weeks, we were carrying those wooden boxes with the ball bearings. You know what a ball bearing is-- it's like what cars, trucks, and things like having in the wheels. And of course, it they didn't give us anything to eat. They didn't give us any money. But we got-- I got some money. And I bought some basic food. And it went on for about six weeks. After six weeks, we were taken by train to the Austro-Hungarian border where they were keeping us in a kind of combination concentration camp and-- and-- and forced-labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the transfer?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're nearly out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
